prompt: Explain the attention mechanism in transformer models to a software engineer who has never done ML. Keep it to about 200 words.
response:
Think of a sentence as a list of tokens. A transformer turns each token into a vector (an embedding), then uses attention to decide how much each token should “look at” every other token to compute its next representation.

Mechanically, each token is projected into three vectors:
- Query (Q): what this token is looking for
- Key (K): what this token offers as a match signal
- Value (V): the information to pass along

For one token, attention computes a similarity score between its Q and every other token’s K (dot products). Those scores are normalized (softmax) to weights that sum to 1. The token’s output is a weighted sum of all the V vectors, so it gathers context from the whole sequence. This is self-attention because Q, K, and V all come from the same sequence.

Multi-head attention runs this process in parallel with several independent Q/K/V projections. Each “head” can focus on different relations (e.g., subject-verb, coreference). Outputs from all heads are concatenated and mixed with a small feed-forward network. Positional encodings are added so the model knows order, since attention itself is permutation-invariant.

Key properties:
- Parallel over tokens (faster training than RNNs)
- Captures long-range dependencies
- Cost scales roughly with sequence_length^2 (the attention matrix)
- Cross-attention is the same idea, but Q comes from one sequence and K/V from another (e.g., encoder-decoder).
